title: The Algorithm
summary: A finite, exact procedure ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù a recipe of steps that, followed mechanically, produces a result. Algorithms predate computers by more than a millennium, but the word acquired a second life as the name for the opaque ranking, recommendation, and decision systems that now sort social feeds, set prices, screen job applicants, and allocate risk across modern life.

title: The Abbasid Caliphate
summary: The Islamic caliphate that ruled from Baghdad from 750 to 1258 CE ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù overthrowing the Umayyads through a revolutionary movement, governing the largest empire of its era, presiding over the Islamic Golden Age of science, philosophy, and medicine, and ending with the Mongol sack of Baghdad that destroyed the caliphate's political and cultural center.

title: The Abolition Movement
summary: The organized campaign to end the transatlantic slave trade and slavery itself, spanning roughly a century from the 1780s through Brazilian emancipation in 1888.

title: Abraham Lincoln
summary: The sixteenth president of the United States, who held the Union together through civil war, issued the Emancipation Proclamation, and was assassinated five days after the Confederate surrender ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù and whose use of war powers, redefinition of the American founding, and management of an unwinnable political coalition established the modern American presidency.

title: Accounting
summary: The discipline of recording, classifying, and communicating economic activity ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù and the technology through which power has made the economic world visible to itself. Accounting produced the account, double-entry bookkeeping, the audit, the profession, and the idea of accountability itself, each extending the reach of legibility over economic life.

title: Adam Smith
summary: Scottish moral philosopher whose Wealth of Nations (1776) provided the foundational analysis of market economies, the division of labor, and the case for free trade ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù and whose Theory of Moral Sentiments (1759) grounded economic behavior in a broader account of human sympathy and ethics.

title: The Agricultural Revolution
summary: The transition from hunter-gatherer foraging to settled farming ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù occurring independently in at least seven regions across ten thousand years ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù that produced surplus food, population growth, cities, states, writing, and social hierarchy, and that Jared Diamond called 'the worst mistake in the history of the human race' even as it made civilization possible.

title: Aksum
summary: The ancient kingdom of northern Ethiopia and Eritrea that became one of the ancient world's great trading powers, one of the first states to adopt Christianity as a state religion, and the origin point of a living tradition ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù the Ethiopian Orthodox Church ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù that claims an unbroken line to the fourth century.

title: Alan Turing
summary: The British mathematician who founded theoretical computer science, broke the Enigma cipher during the Second World War, and asked 'Can machines think?' ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù defining the questions that artificial intelligence has pursued ever since ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù before being prosecuted for homosexuality and dying at 41.

title: Alexander the Great
summary: The Macedonian king who conquered the largest contiguous empire in ancient history in just thirteen years -- from Greece to Egypt to Persia to the edge of India -- and whose campaigns spread Greek culture across the Near East in ways that shaped the ancient world for centuries.

title: Alexandria
summary: The city Alexander the Great founded at the mouth of the Nile in 331 BCE ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù which became the ancient world's greatest center of knowledge, the meeting point of Greek, Egyptian, Jewish, and later Christian and Islamic intellectual traditions, and the site of the most famous library ever destroyed

title: Al-Khwarizmi
summary: The ninth-century Persian scholar at Baghdad's House of Wisdom whose treatise on al-jabr gave algebra its name and its method, whose name became the word 'algorithm,' and who helped carry the Hindu-Arabic decimal system into the Islamic world and eventually into Europe ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù making him one of the most consequential mathematicians in history, despite almost nothing being known about his life.

title: B.R. Ambedkar
summary: The Dalit scholar, jurist, and political leader who designed the Indian constitution, mounted the most powerful intellectual challenge to Gandhi's approach to caste, converted to Buddhism with hundreds of thousands of followers, and whose arguments about structural inequality and the limits of reform from within remain the foundational texts of Dalit liberation thought.

title: The American Civil War
summary: The war fought between the United States of America and the Confederate States of America from 1861 to 1865, in which the UnionΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβÄû¬Δs victory ended slavery in America and settled the question of whether states could secede.

title: The American Revolution
summary: The colonial revolt that established the first modern republic, created the template for constitutional self-government, produced founding documents whose contradictions between universal rights and racial slavery structured American politics for a century, and became the reference point for anti-colonial movements across two centuries

title: The Analects
summary: The collected sayings and conversations of Confucius, compiled by his disciples after his death. The Analects is the foundational text of Confucianism and the most influential book in East Asian history ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù not primarily as philosophy but as a technology of governance. For fourteen centuries, the civil service examination system that governed China, Korea, Vietnam, and Japan required mastery of the Analects and its commentaries.

title: Ancient Egypt
summary: The civilization that arose from the Nile's predictable floods ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù organized around the most reliable agricultural system in the ancient world, producing the administrative, artistic, and engineering achievements that made Egypt a continuous civilization for three thousand years and a model for every subsequent empire that encountered it

title: Angkor
summary: The vast medieval capital of the Khmer Empire in what is now Cambodia ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù a sprawling low-density city built around an engineered network of reservoirs and canals, home to Angkor Wat and the Bayon, abandoned as a royal seat in the 15th century, rediscovered by Europeans in the 19th, and now both Cambodia's national symbol and one of Asia's most visited ruins.

title: Antibiotics
summary: Compounds that kill or inhibit the growth of bacteria, first developed from natural mold extracts in the 1920sΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™1940s and now among the most widely used medicines in human history ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù responsible for saving hundreds of millions of lives, enabling modern surgery and cancer treatment, and generating the crisis of antimicrobial resistance that threatens to reverse their gains.

title: Apartheid
summary: The system of institutionalized racial segregation and white minority rule in South Africa, formally in force from 1948 to 1994, which classified every person by race, organized every aspect of social and economic life accordingly, suppressed Black political resistance with systematic violence, and ended through negotiated transition after decades of struggle.

title: Aristotle
summary: The philosopher who invented the categories through which Western and global intellectual culture organized knowledge for two millennia

title: Artificial Intelligence
summary: The project of building machines that can perform tasks requiring human-like intelligence ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù now the most rapidly developing technology in human history and the subject of the most consequential unresolved questions about the future of work, knowledge, and civilization.

title: The Art of War
period: c. 5th century BCE (traditional); possibly c. 4thΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™3rd century BCE
summary: A brief military treatise attributed to the Chinese strategist Sun Tzu ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù the foundational text of East Asian military thought and the most widely applied strategic framework outside its original military context in world history.

title: Ashoka
summary: Emperor of the Maurya Empire who conquered most of the Indian subcontinent, was transformed by the violence of his wars, converted to Buddhism, and then ruled according to dhamma ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù a code of ethical governance ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù leaving behind the earliest major inscriptions in Indian history and one of the most influential examples of a ruler renouncing conquest.

title: Athenian Democracy
summary: The first sustained experiment in democratic rule ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù more radical and participatory than any modern democracy, built on slavery, and foundational to Western political thought.

title: Athens
summary: The city that became Western civilization's origin myth ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù and the historical reality that the myth requires not looking at too carefully

title: Atomic Theory
summary: The idea that matter is composed of indivisible fundamental units ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù atoms ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù proposed by Greek philosophers, abandoned for two millennia, revived by chemists in the 19th century, transformed by physicists in the 20th, and now the foundation of all chemistry, much of physics, and the technology of nuclear weapons and nuclear power.

title: Augustine of Hippo
summary: North African theologian and philosopher whose Confessions and City of God shaped Western Christianity's understanding of sin, grace, free will, time, and the relationship between the church and political power ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù making him the most influential Christian thinker between Paul and the Reformation.

title: Authoritarianism
summary: The political system or disposition in which power is concentrated in a single leader or small group, political opposition is suppressed, and citizens are subordinated to the state ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù a category that encompasses regimes from military juntas to competitive autocracies, and a problem that democracy has never fully solved.

title: Aviation
summary: Powered, controlled flight ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù first achieved at Kitty Hawk in 1903 and scaled within a single human lifetime into a global network of jet routes carrying billions of passengers a year. Aviation shrank the planet from weeks to hours, restructured warfare, enabled mass tourism and migration, and built the fast lane of globalization in air freight ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù while generating roughly three percent of global warming emissions and remaining dependent on state subsidy and military procurement.

title: The Axial Age
summary: The period between roughly 800 and 200 BCE when, independently and without evident contact, thinkers in China, India, Iran, and the Greek and Hebrew worlds developed the philosophical, religious, and ethical frameworks that still organize human civilization -- Confucianism, Buddhism, Zoroastrianism, philosophical monotheism, and Greek philosophy all emerging within a few centuries of each other.

title: The Aztec Empire
summary: The Aztec Triple Alliance (c. 1428ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™1521) built a tributary empire, a sophisticated water-management infrastructure, and one of the largest cities in the world ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù before the Spanish conquest reduced it to rubble within two years. The entry asks what the Aztec state actually was as a political-economic structure, independent of how it ended.

title: The Balance Sheet
summary: The accounting statement that captures what an entity owns and what it owes at a single moment in time ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù and, in doing so, became the foundational technology through which capital was measured, compared, and mobilized across the modern world.

title: Banking
summary: The institutional practice of holding deposits, extending credit, and transferring money across space and time ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù a foundation of economic civilization so pervasive that its absence is almost unimaginable, and whose periodic crises reveal how much of modern life is built on organized debt.

title: The Banknote
summary: The paper promise-to-pay that became money itself ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù a printed instrument whose value rests not on the worth of its material but on the trust that it can be redeemed or accepted by others, from the jiaozi of Song-dynasty China to the fiat currencies of today.

title: Bankruptcy
summary: The legal procedure for resolving debts that cannot be paid ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù the orderly, collective alternative to debtors' prison, flight, or creditor free-for-all ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù and the institution that, by letting economic failure end without ending the person, made risk-taking capitalism possible.

title: The Bazaar
summary: The bazaar ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù souk, covered market, urban caravanserai node ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù is not merely a place to buy things but an institution organized around relationships, reputation, and trust rather than fixed prices and anonymous exchange. From Istanbul's Grand Bazaar to Isfahan's Royal Bazaar to Cairo's Khan el-Khalili, these dense commercial spaces ran on guilds, extended credit, face-to-face bargaining, and the social weight of a merchant's name.

title: Beijing
period: c. 1045 BCE ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ present (major city); 1421 ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ present (as capital)
summary: The capital of China for six centuries and the center of the world's most continuously inhabited civilization ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù a city whose built environment embeds a complete cosmological and political order and whose contemporary significance is inseparable from the authoritarian state that now occupies it.

title: Beowulf
summary: The Old English epic poem preserved in a single manuscript from around 1000 CE, recounting the hero Beowulf's battles against monsters and a dragon. The oldest surviving long poem in English and the foundational text of English literary tradition, its reception history ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù from obscurity through nineteenth-century nationalism to Tolkien's critical transformation and Seamus Heaney's landmark 2000 translation ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù is as significant as the poem itself.

title: The Bhagavad Gita
period: c. 200 BCE ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ 200 CE (composition); embedded in Mahabharata c. 400 BCE ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ 400 CE
summary: A philosophical dialogue embedded in the Indian epic Mahabharata, in which the god Krishna instructs the warrior Arjuna on duty, action, and the nature of the self ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù the foundational text of Hindu ethical and spiritual thought and one of the most widely read philosophical works in human history.

title: The Bible
summary: The compilation of texts ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù assembled over roughly a thousand years of composition, canonized through centuries of religious politics, translated into every major language, and used to justify slavery, liberation, empire, resistance, persecution, and redemption ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù that remains the most widely distributed and most contested book in human history

title: The Black Death
summary: The most lethal pandemic in recorded history ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù and the catastrophe that revealed what medieval European civilization was built on, and what it wasn't

title: The Bond Market
summary: The marketplace where governments and corporations borrow by selling tradeable promises to repay with interest, and where the price of that borrowing is continuously set. The bond market turns a loan into a security that can change hands, prices a government's credibility every day, and exercises a discipline over states that the stock market does not ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù which is why it has often mattered more than the exchange where shares trade.

title: Borders
summary: A border is the line where one sovereignΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβÄû¬Δs authority ends and anotherΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβÄû¬Δs begins ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù and the site of a deep moral argument over who may draw it, cross it, and be excluded by it. Borders organize the modern world into territorial states, turn accidents of birth into lifelong allocations of rights, and generate one of the most genuinely contested debates in contemporary ethics: whether any government has the right to exclude outsiders, and on what grounds.

title: The British Empire
summary: The largest empire in history by land area, encompassing at its peak in the 1920s approximately 25% of the worldΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβÄû¬Δs land surface and governing roughly 458 million people.

title: The Bronze Age Collapse
summary: The near-simultaneous destruction of every major civilization in the eastern Mediterranean around 1200 BCE ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù the Mycenaeans, the Hittites, the Egyptians, the Ugaritic trading city ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù ending the first great age of international trade and complex palace economies, and remaining one of history's most studied and least explained catastrophes.

title: Buddhism
summary: The religious and philosophical tradition founded on the teachings of Siddhartha Gautama ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù the Buddha ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù in northeastern India around the fifth century BCE, whose analysis of suffering and the path to its cessation became one of the most consequential intellectual traditions in world history, spreading across Asia and now globally.

title: Bureaucracy
summary: The system of administration through specialized officials, written records, hierarchical authority, and impersonal rules ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù independently invented by every large civilization in history, from Han China to the Roman Empire to the Ottoman devshirme to the modern nation-state ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù and the concept through which Max Weber understood modernity itself: the 'iron cage' of rationalized, impersonal rule that modern societies cannot escape.

title: The Byzantine Empire
summary: The eastern continuation of the Roman Empire ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù centered on Constantinople, Christian in religion, Greek in language and culture, Roman in legal tradition and political identity ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù that survived the fall of the Western Empire by a thousand years, preserved and transmitted classical learning, shaped the civilizations of Eastern Europe and Russia, and fell to the Ottoman Turks in 1453, an event that shook the Christian world and sent Greek scholars westward to fuel the Renaissance.

title: Cahokia
period: c. 1050 ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ 1350 CE (Mississippian florescence); abandoned by c. 1400 CE
summary: The largest city in the Americas north of Mexico before European contact ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù a planned earthwork metropolis near modern St. Louis that rose around 1050 CE, held tens of thousands of people, and was abandoned within a few centuries, complicating the story that pre-contact North America held no cities.

title: The Canal
summary: The constructed waterway ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù dug, embanked, and locked ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù through which civilizations have moved grain, goods, and armies for six thousand years. Canals come in two great families: irrigation channels that carry water to crops, and navigation canals that carry boats across terrain. Both are acts of will against geography, enormously expensive to build and maintain, and transformative where they succeed ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù rewriting the logic of distance, making agriculture possible in dry lands, and collapsing oceans for the ships that connect the modern world.

title: Capitalism
summary: The economic system in which the means of production are privately owned, goods and services are allocated through markets, and profit-seeking drives investment ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù a system that has generated unprecedented material wealth, lifted billions from poverty, and produced inequality, environmental destruction, and periodic crises that have generated the most consequential political conflicts of the modern era.

title: Capital
summary: Karl Marx's three-volume analysis of the capitalist mode of production which provided the theoretical foundation for socialist and communist movements worldwide. Capital argued that capitalism systematically exploits labour through surplus value extraction, and that capitalism contains internal contradictions producing eventual crisis. The book's world effect ran through the states and movements it founded rather than through academic economics.

title: Carthage
period: c. 814 BCE ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ 146 BCE (Punic); 44 BCE ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ 698 CE (Roman and late antique)
summary: The Phoenician trading city that became Rome's most dangerous rival and was completely destroyed ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù the paradigm case of the losers' civilization in Western historical memory, known almost entirely through the accounts of those who destroyed it.

title: The Census
summary: The systematic counting and categorization of populations by the state ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù which began in ancient Mesopotamia and Egypt as a tool of taxation and military conscription, became the instrument through which modern states know, administer, and govern their populations, and reveals in its categories (race, religion, language, occupation) how states define who belongs, who counts, and on what terms.

title: Central Banking
summary: The institution that sits above the banks ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù issuer of the national currency, lender of last resort when a panic threatens to bring the system down, and setter of the interest rate that prices borrowing across an economy. The central bank manages the value of money, and its power has grown enormously even as its legitimacy stays contested.

title: Charlemagne
summary: The Frankish king crowned Emperor of the Romans on Christmas Day 800 CE who unified most of Western Europe under a single ruler for the first time since the Roman Empire's fall, promoted literacy and learning in the Carolingian Renaissance, and whose empire ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù despite fragmenting within a generation of his death ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù became the template for European political and religious identity that persisted for a thousand years.

title: The Chernobyl Disaster
summary: The explosion and fire at Reactor No. 4 of the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant in Soviet Ukraine on April 26, 1986 ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù the worst nuclear-power accident in history ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù which killed dozens in the immediate aftermath and released radioactive contamination across Europe, forcing the permanent evacuation of Pripyat, exposing the Soviet system's institutional pathologies, and accelerating the political dynamics that would end the USSR by 1991.

title: Cholera
summary: The acute diarrheal disease caused by Vibrio cholerae that spread in seven global pandemics from 1817 to the present, killing millions and driving the sanitary reforms that created modern public health infrastructure. Cholera is the founding disease of epidemiology: John Snow's 1854 investigation established the field's core methods before germ theory existed.

title: Christianity
summary: The religious movement that began as a Jewish sect in Roman Palestine, became the official religion of the Roman Empire, shaped Western civilization's law, art, philosophy, and political thought for two thousand years, and now claims more than two billion adherents worldwide ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù the largest and most geographically diverse religion in history.

title: Cicero
summary: The Roman orator, lawyer, politician, and philosopher who served as consul in 63 BCE, wrote the foundational Latin works on rhetoric, political philosophy, and moral philosophy, and was executed in 43 BCE on Mark Antony's orders ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù leaving a body of work that was rediscovered in the 14th century by Petrarch and became the intellectual foundation of Renaissance humanism, early modern republicanism, and the Western rhetorical tradition.

title: Citizenship
summary: Citizenship is the political technology that divides humanity into members and non-members of a state. It grants rights to some and withholds them from others. Understanding citizenship requires understanding not just who belongs but why the boundary exists, what it costs those outside it, and who has historically been expelled from the category.

title: The Civil Rights Movement
summary: The organized campaign by African Americans between 1954 and 1968 that dismantled legal segregation and won voting rights through nonviolent direct action ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù achieving landmark legislation while revealing the limits of legal reform in addressing structural racial inequality.

title: Cleopatra VII
summary: The last ruler of Ptolemaic Egypt ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù Greek by dynasty, Egyptian by cultural identity and political strategy ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù who allied with Julius Caesar and Mark Antony in the dying decades of the Roman Republic, presided over Egypt's absorption into the Roman Empire, and became one of the most mythologized figures in Western history, her actual political genius largely obscured by two thousand years of sexual legend.

title: Climate Change
summary: The ongoing warming of the Earth's climate system driven by human greenhouse gas emissions ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù the defining environmental force of the present and future

title: Coal
summary: The fossilized sunlight that powered the Industrial Revolution ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù by providing the concentrated energy that broke the organic economy's land constraint, created the working class, built the railway network, and began accumulating the atmospheric carbon that is now reshaping the planet's climate

title: The Code of Hammurabi
summary: The collection of 282 laws carved onto a black basalt stele by the Babylonian king Hammurabi around 1754 BCE ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù one of the oldest and most complete legal texts to survive from the ancient world ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù that established graduated punishments based on class, codified the principle of proportional retribution, and presented law as a gift from the gods entrusted to a just king.

title: The Cold War
summary: The geopolitical condition of superpower standoff that structured international relations for four decades ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù and felt, to those inside it, like it would never end

title: Colonialism
summary: The establishment and maintenance of political and economic control by one society over another ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù the system through which European powers between the 15th and 20th centuries dominated most of the world's surface, extracting resources, displacing populations, restructuring economies, and imposing administrative systems whose consequences persist long after formal colonial rule ended.

title: The Columbian Exchange
summary: The biological and cultural transfer between the Old World and the New World that followed Columbus's 1492 voyage ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù connecting previously isolated ecologies through the movement of crops, animals, diseases, and peoples, producing one of the most catastrophic population collapses in human history and simultaneously reshaping the agricultural foundations of civilization on every inhabited continent

title: The Commons
summary: A resource held and governed collectively ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù neither privately owned nor state-controlled ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù covering medieval English pastures, ocean fisheries, the atmosphere, open-source software, and the digital knowledge pool. Long dismissed as inherently prone to ruin, the commons was rehabilitated by Elinor Ostrom's Nobel Prize-winning research showing that communities can and do govern shared resources sustainably, making the commons a serious third option alongside private property and the state.

title: Communism
summary: The political and economic theory holding that private ownership of the means of production should be abolished and replaced by common ownership, with the goal of a classless society ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù and the name for the revolutionary movements and states that have attempted to build it.

title: Computing
summary: The infrastructure for processing and transforming information at scale ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù from Babbage's mechanical difference engine through the first electronic computers to the networked systems that now mediate commerce, communication, science, and government worldwide. Computing is the story of how humanity built infrastructure that amplifies information processing as roads amplify movement and electricity amplifies energy.

title: Concrete
summary: The composite building material ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù a mixture of aggregate, water, and a binding agent ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù that has been the primary construction medium of every major civilization since Rome, and whose modern form, reinforced with steel, is the material substrate of the contemporary built world: its dams, bridges, highways, apartment blocks, and infrastructure.

title: Confucius
summary: The Chinese philosopher whose teachings on moral cultivation, proper relationships, and the virtuous ruler became the intellectual foundation of East Asian political culture for two and a half millennia ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù and whose ideas have never stopped being contested, weaponized, and reinterpreted

title: Constantinople
summary: The city founded by Constantine in 330 CE on the strait between Europe and Asia that served as the capital of the Roman Empire, then the Byzantine Empire for over a millennium, before falling to the Ottoman Turks in 1453 and becoming Istanbul.

title: Constitution
summary: A constitution is a technology for binding power ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù a set of rules that today's government must follow because yesterday's decisions made them binding. The central puzzle is how this can work at all: if the sovereign is supreme, who enforces the limits on the sovereign?

title: The Contract
summary: The legal and social institution through which two or more parties create binding obligations by mutual agreement ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù the mechanism that organizes most of market society, that has been extended metaphorically to explain political obligation and social order, and that illuminates the conditions under which voluntary exchange is genuinely voluntary.

title: The Cooperative
summary: An enterprise owned and governed by its members rather than outside shareholders ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù the organizational form that asks whether democratic self-governance can survive the pressures of market competition, and that has generated an alternative tradition of economic thinking running alongside capitalism for nearly two centuries.

title: Copper
summary: The first metal worked by human hands ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù soft enough to shape without smelting, abundant enough to spread across cultures, and the base element of bronze ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù whose adoption reorganized human labor, warfare, and trade across the ancient world and whose descendants still wire the electrical infrastructure of the modern one.

title: The Corporation
summary: The legal form that allows a group of investors to pool capital under a single artificial person ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù shielding them from personal liability, enabling the accumulation of wealth at a scale no individual could sustain, and producing an institution whose power to shape law, labor, and politics has made it the defining economic structure of the modern world.

title: Cotton

title: The Enlightenment
summary: The 18th-century intellectual movement that made reason the supreme authority ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù and whose legacy is still being fought over

title: Fire

title: The French Revolution

summary: The strategic doctrine that nuclear war can be prevented by ensuring that any nuclear attack would trigger a retaliatory strike capable of inflicting unacceptable damage on the attacker ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù making the initiation of nuclear war irrational for any state, and thereby achieving peace through the permanent mutual threat of civilizational destruction.

title: The Odyssey
summary: An ancient Greek epic poem attributed to Homer, composed around the 8th century BCE in the tradition of oral poetry, following Odysseus on his ten-year journey home after the Trojan War -- a foundational work of Western literature whose narrative templates have been reworked in every subsequent century.

title: Oil
summary: The fossil fuel that powers the modern world economy -- extracted commercially since 1859, the foundation of transportation, agriculture, plastics, and warfare for over a century, and the primary driver of anthropogenic climate change.

title: The Olmec
summary: The earliest major civilization of Mesoamerica, centered on the hot, wet lowlands of the Gulf Coast of Mexico from roughly 1500 to 400 BCE. The Olmec built the first large ceremonial centers in the region, first at San Lorenzo and later at La Venta, and carved colossal basalt heads weighing many tons from stone hauled across long distances. They worked jade with great skill and left behind a recurring set of religious images, including a part-human, part-jaguar being. Whether they were the single "mother culture" that shaped everything that followed in Mesoamerica, or one of several early "sister cultures," is still debated.

title: The Opium Wars
summary: Two conflicts between Britain and China (1839ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™1842 and 1856ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™1860) fought over British insistence on the right to sell opium in China and China's attempts to prohibit the trade. The Opium Wars inaugurated China's century of humiliation, opened Chinese markets and territory to foreign powers through unequal treaties, and remain defining events in Chinese national memory and foreign policy.

title: On the Origin of Species
summary: The foundational document of modern biology ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù and the most culturally contested scientific text in history

title: The Ottoman Empire
summary: The Turkic Muslim empire that ruled Anatolia, the Middle East, North Africa, and southeastern Europe for six centuries ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù the longest-lasting Islamic empire and the context for much of the contemporary Middle East.

title: Pablo Picasso
summary: The Spanish painter and sculptor who co-invented Cubism, transformed what Western art could look like, and became the most commercially dominant artist of the twentieth century

title: Paper
summary: The surface on which civilization recorded itself ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù invented in China around 100 BCE, transmitted westward through the Islamic world, and eventually combined with movable type to produce the information revolution that made the modern world; now being displaced by digital surfaces whose consequences are still unfolding

title: The Parliament
summary: The representative assembly through which a society makes its laws, decides its taxes, controls its spending, and holds its rulers to account through open debate and recorded votes ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù an institution that grew out of medieval councils summoned to grant a ruler money, won the long struggle over the power of the purse, shifted from advising a monarch to governing in his place, and then spread across the world as the working machinery of representative government, carrying both its promise of consent and its persistent failures of inclusion.

title: Passports
summary: The small booklet that ties a person to a state and governs their right to cross borders ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù converting citizenship from a legal status into a portable document, and turning movement across the earth from a natural act into a privilege that states may grant or deny. From ancient letters of safe-conduct to the World War I checkpoint and the biometric chip, the passport is the stateΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβÄû¬Δs monopoly on authorizing human mobility made physical.

title: The Patent
summary: A temporary, state-granted monopoly over an invention, given in exchange for public disclosure of how it works ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù a deliberate bargain between the inventor's reward and the public's freedom to use knowledge, and one of the most contested instruments of innovation policy.

title: The Persian Empire
summary: The Achaemenid Persian Empire ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù founded by Cyrus the Great around 550 BCE and destroyed by Alexander the Great in 330 BCE ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù was the largest empire the ancient world had yet seen, stretching from the Aegean to the Indus, governing perhaps 40 percent of the world's population through a system of administrative tolerance, royal roads, and satrapal governance that became the template for empire across the ancient world.

title: Petrarch
summary: The Italian poet and scholar who invented the concept of historical distance ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù the idea that ancient Rome was a distinct civilization that had ended, not simply a continuous past ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù and whose recovery of classical texts and sense of historical belatedness made the Renaissance possible as an intellectual project.

title: The Plantation
summary: The agricultural-industrial system that organized enslaved and coerced labor to mass-produce cash crops ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù sugar, tobacco, cotton, coffee ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù for distant markets. The plantation was not a farm but a factory in the field: a regime of gang labor, surveillance, and violence that ran on a precise calendar, generated immense wealth for owners and the economies they fed, built racially ordered slave societies across the Atlantic world, and left long afterlives in sharecropping, the convict-lease farm, and the company estate.

title: Plato
summary: The Athenian philosopher whose dialogues invented Western philosophy's central questions ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù the nature of knowledge, justice, beauty, and the good life ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù and whose theory of Forms, defense of philosopher-kings, and suspicion of democracy have been argued with, built upon, and reacted against for 2,400 years

title: Populism
summary: The political style and ideology that pits a virtuous people against a corrupt elite ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù a form of politics that has appeared across the left-right spectrum, in democratic and authoritarian contexts, and in every region of the world ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù and one of the most contested concepts in contemporary political science, not least because it is simultaneously a diagnosis of democratic dysfunction and its symptom.

title: The Port
summary: The institution through which maritime commerce, migration, disease, and ideas moved between worlds ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù a physical node of docks and warehouses, a regulatory space of customs and quarantine, a cosmopolitan contact zone where strangers met on commercial terms, and a labor system whose containerization revolution in the mid-20th century remade global trade and displaced the dockworker communities that had defined port cities for centuries.

title: The Post Office
summary: The state-run network that carries written messages and parcels to every address at a uniform price ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù the first communication infrastructure to bind a whole territory together. Built on ancient royal courier relays, transformed by Rowland Hill's Penny Post of 1840 and the prepaid postage stamp, the postal system created a national reading public, funded mail-order commerce, pioneered the postal money order and savings bank, and gave rise to the address system itself. It was also a tool of the state: officials read the mail long before privacy law protected it. The template every later communication network was measured against.

title: Power
summary: The concept that makes the invisible architecture of social life visible ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù and reveals why authority persists even when it could be challenged

title: The Invention of the Printing Press
summary: The technology that made information cheap to reproduce ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù and institutional monopoly on knowledge permanently impossible

title: The Prison
summary: The modern institution that punishes by depriving people of liberty over measured time ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù a relatively recent invention that replaced the public, bodily punishments of earlier eras with confinement behind walls. The prison promised humane, rational reform and grew instead into a vast machinery of confinement whose results are deeply contested, reaching its most extreme form in the mass incarceration of the late-twentieth-century United States.

title: Probability
summary: The mathematics of uncertainty ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù which transformed how civilization manages risk, enabled the insurance industry, statistical medicine, quantum mechanics, and machine learning, and produced an intellectual foreclosure that made prior frameworks for reasoning about uncertain futures permanently inadequate

title: Propaganda
summary: The deliberate manipulation of beliefs, emotions, and perceptions through selective presentation of information ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù a practice as old as organized power and a concept whose modern form was shaped by the First World War, theorized by Freud's nephew Edward Bernays, industrialized by totalitarian states, and then so thoroughly absorbed into advertising, public relations, and electoral politics that its boundaries with legitimate persuasion became genuinely contested.

title: Property
summary: Property is not a thing but a relationship ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù a bundle of enforceable claims against everyone else about who can use, exclude others from, and transfer something. Understanding how a claim becomes ownership, and what kinds of things can be owned, is one of the most consequential questions in political and economic life.

title: The Protestant Reformation
summary: The 16th-century religious movement that fractured Western Christianity, produced the modern state system, and demonstrated what the printing press could do

title: Public Debt
summary: The money a state borrows against its future taxes ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù the device that has let governments spend far beyond what they collect, funding wars, infrastructure, and ambitions no single year of revenue could cover. Public debt turns a government's promise to repay into the bedrock of modern finance, which makes the question of whose promises are trusted, and who ultimately pays, one of the central problems of state power.

title: Public Health
summary: The organized effort of societies to protect and improve the health of populations as a whole, rather than treating individuals one at a time. Public health rests on the insight that the conditions in which people live ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù water, air, housing, food, income ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù shape health outcomes more powerfully than medicine alone.

title: The Qing Dynasty
summary: The last imperial dynasty of China, founded by the Manchu Aisin Gioro clan in 1644 and ruling for 268 years until its collapse in 1912. At its height, the Qing Empire was the largest state in Chinese history and among the most populous on Earth, controlling a territory that included present-day China, Mongolia, Tibet, and Xinjiang.

title: Quantum Mechanics
summary: The physical theory developed between 1900 and 1930 that describes the behavior of matter and energy at atomic and subatomic scales ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù replacing classical physics in that domain, resisting intuitive interpretation for a century, and underpinning virtually all modern technology from semiconductors to lasers to medical imaging.

title: The Quran
summary: The central religious text of Islam, believed by Muslims to be the direct word of God revealed to the Prophet Muhammad. The Quran is the foundation of Islamic theology, law, and ethics; the model for classical Arabic; the text memorized in its entirety by millions of hafiz; and the constitution of Islamic civilization in a sense that goes beyond any analogy to scripture in other traditions.

title: Rabbinic Judaism
summary: The form of Judaism that emerged after the Roman destruction of the Second Temple in 70 CE ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù replacing Temple sacrifice with prayer, priestly authority with rabbinic scholarship, and territorial religion with a portable legal and intellectual system that could survive anywhere on earth.

title: Race
summary: The concept of dividing humanity into distinct biological groups based on physical characteristics ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù a system of classification whose scientific validity is rejected by contemporary biology but whose social and political consequences have been, and remain, thoroughly real.

title: Radio
summary: The technology that sent sound through the air without wires ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù and by reaching everyone at once, created the first electronic broadcast mass medium and the modern broadcast nation. Radio collapsed the distance between a single voice and millions of listeners, making it possible, for the first time, for one person to speak intimately to a whole country simultaneously. That power built democratic movements and fueled dictatorships alike.

title: Railways
summary: The network of iron and steel tracks that reorganized the movement of goods, people, and armies across land surfaces, collapsing distance and time in ways that restructured economies, empires, and daily life. Railways did not merely accelerate existing patterns of movement ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù they created new geographies of production, settlement, and power.

title: The Renaissance
summary: The cultural and intellectual movement that transformed European art, literature, philosophy, and science between roughly 1300 and 1600 -- centered in Italian city-states, organized through patronage, and driven by the recovery and celebration of ancient Greek and Roman learning.

title: Revolution
summary: The analytical concept describing the sudden, fundamental transformation of a political system ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù typically through popular mobilization, the overthrow of existing authority, and the seizure of state power ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù and the framework scholars use to explain why revolutions happen, how they develop, and what they produce.

title: Rice
summary: The crop that fed half of humanity ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù domesticated in China's Yangtze River valley around 7000 BCE, spread across Asia through millennia of agricultural development, and shaped the labor-intensive paddy civilizations of East and Southeast Asia whose demographic weight, political structures, and cultural patterns remain distinct from the wheat-based civilizations of the West

title: Roads
summary: The technology of surfaced paths connecting human settlements ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù one of the oldest and most consequential investments any civilization makes, the infrastructure through which trade, armies, messages, and ideas move, and a technology that has organized the political geography of every complex society from ancient Mesopotamia to the modern highway system.

title: The Roman Empire
period: 27 BCE ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ 476 CE (Western); 27 BCE ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™ 1453 CE (Eastern)
summary: The political structure that unified the Mediterranean world for five centuries, produced the legal, administrative, and infrastructural inheritance on which European and Near Eastern civilization was built, and whose legacy ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù in law, language, religion, and institutional imagination ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù has never fully ended

title: The Roman Republic
summary: The political system through which Rome governed itself for nearly five centuries ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù replacing monarchy with elected magistrates, a senate, and popular assemblies ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù before collapsing into the civil wars that produced the empire, and bequeathing its institutional vocabulary to every subsequent republic.

title: Romanticism
summary: The intellectual and artistic movement that arose as a reaction against Enlightenment rationalism ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù emphasizing emotion, imagination, nature, and the individual ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù whose influence on art, literature, music, philosophy, and politics has been so pervasive that we still live within it.

title: Rome
summary: The city on the Tiber that ruled an empire, became the seat of a universal Church, was sacked and rebuilt multiple times, and remains the most continuously symbolically contested location in the Western world ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù a place that every subsequent civilization has claimed, imitated, mourned, or defined itself against.

title: Jean-Jacques Rousseau
summary: The Geneva-born philosopher whose ideas about natural goodness, the social contract, the general will, and education shaped the French Revolution, Romanticism, modern democracy, and progressive pedagogy ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù while his life and contradictions made him one of the most contested figures in Western thought.

title: The Russian Revolution
summary: The collapse of Tsarist Russia in 1917 and the Bolshevik seizure of power ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù producing, after a devastating civil war, the Soviet Union, the world's first communist state, and reshaping global politics for the remainder of the 20th century.

title: The Rwandan Genocide
summary: The organized mass killing of Rwanda's Tutsi minority and moderate Hutu by the Hutu-dominated government and its militia networks between April and July 1994 ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù approximately 800,000 people killed in 100 days, the fastest genocide in recorded history, carried out with machetes and local administrative structures while the United Nations force present in Rwanda watched and the world's governments deliberately avoided the word 'genocide' to evade the obligation to act.

title: Salt
summary: The mineral sodium chloride ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù essential for human survival, one of the oldest trade commodities in the world, a driver of civilizational development across every inhabited continent, and a substance whose history illuminates how the most basic biological necessities become instruments of commerce, taxation, and political power.

title: Scholasticism
summary: The dominant intellectual method of medieval European universities from roughly 1050 to 1350, organized around the systematic reconciliation of Christian theology with ancient philosophy ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù above all Aristotle ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù through rigorous logical analysis. Scholasticism produced the first institutionalized tradition of rational inquiry in the Western world.

title: The Scientific Revolution
summary: A transformation in how European thinkers understood and investigated the natural world ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù running from Copernicus's heliocentric model of the solar system to Newton's laws of motion ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù that established the experimental method, mathematical description of nature, and the idea of a law-governed universe as the foundations of modern science.

title: The Scramble for Africa
summary: The rapid partition of Africa by European powers between roughly 1880 and 1914 ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù in which a continent that was 10% European-controlled became 90% European-controlled in three decades ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù establishing borders that ignored existing political and ethnic realities and creating the colonial structures whose legacy continues to define African politics.

title: Secularism
summary: Secularism is not the absence of religion from public life. It is a family of political arrangements for managing disagreement about ultimate truth. Different societies have answered the same question ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù what do we do when citizens disagree about what God requires? ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù in structurally different ways. Understanding those differences is more analytically useful than treating secularism as a single thing.

title: The September 11 Attacks
summary: The al-Qaeda attacks of September 11, 2001 ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù four hijacked airliners, the collapse of the World Trade Center's twin towers, a strike on the Pentagon, and a fourth plane forced down in Pennsylvania ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù killing roughly 2,977 people and triggering two decades of American-led wars, the construction of a domestic surveillance and security state, and a global transformation of how governments justified coercion in the name of safety.

title: Sewers
summary: The infrastructure for removing human waste from populated areas ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù one of the oldest public health technologies, a system whose presence or absence has determined whether cities could grow beyond a few thousand people, and whose massive construction in the 19th century transformed European and American cities from places where epidemics were normal into places where they were exceptional.

title: Shakespeare
summary: The English playwright and poet who wrote approximately 37 plays and 154 sonnets between roughly 1590 and 1613, working in the commercial theater of Elizabethan and Jacobean London ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù producing a body of work that has been continuously performed, translated, adapted, and argued over for four centuries, and that now functions as the closest thing the English-speaking world has to a universal literary canon.

title: Shareholder Value
summary: The doctrine that a corporation's single legitimate purpose is to maximize financial returns to its shareholders ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù an idea forged in the economics departments of the 1970s, institutionalized through stock-based pay and hostile takeovers in the 1980s, and now contested by a stakeholder movement arguing that a firm answerable only to its owners produces inequality, short-termism, and damage it was never designed to account for.

title: Shipbuilding
summary: The technology and industry of constructing vessels capable of crossing open water ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù from the dugout canoes of the Neolithic to the ironclad battleships and container carriers of the industrial age ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù that connected continents, moved goods and armies, transmitted religion and disease, and organized the global economic system that still operates today.

title: Siddhartha Gautama
period: c. 563ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™483 BCE (traditional); c. 480ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™400 BCE (revised scholarly dating)
summary: The historical founder of Buddhism, whose analysis of suffering and the conditions of human experience produced one of the most enduring and geographically expansive intellectual traditions in human history.

title: Silk
summary: The lustrous textile produced from silkworm cocoons ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù China's most consequential technological secret, the commodity that named the most important trade route in pre-modern history, and a material whose production, trade, and political symbolism reorganized economies and societies from East Asia to the Mediterranean for three thousand years.

title: The Silk Road
summary: The overland and maritime trade network connecting China to the Mediterranean world ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù the primary channel through which goods, religions, technologies, diseases, and ideas moved across Eurasia for over a millennium.

title: SimΟÉΤ£Ο²¬≥n BolΟÉΤ£Ο²¬≠var
summary: The Venezuelan-born military commander and political leader who liberated most of Spanish South America between 1813 and 1825, governing as president or dictator across Venezuela, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Bolivia ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù and who spent his final years watching the republics he had created fragment, federate against him, and slide toward the caudillo instability that would define Latin American politics for generations.

title: Simone de Beauvoir
summary: French existentialist philosopher whose The Second Sex (1949) provided the twentieth century's foundational analysis of women's oppression ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù arguing that 'woman' is not a biological fact but a social construction imposed by a male-defined world ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù and whose life as a public intellectual, writer, and activist embodied the freedom she theorized.

title: Slavery
summary: The institution of owning human beings as property ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù one of the most widespread and durable institutions in human history, practiced on every inhabited continent, powering the ancient economies of Greece, Rome, and the Near East, and reaching its most systematic modern form in the Atlantic plantation complex that built the wealth of the early capitalist world ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù and one of the central subjects of moral and political philosophy, whose abolition required a reconception of what it means to be human.

title: The Transatlantic Slave Trade
summary: The commercial and imperial system that transported 12.5 million Africans to the Americas over three and a half centuries

title: Smallpox
summary: The disease caused by the Variola virus that killed an estimated 300 million people in the 20th century alone, shaped the outcome of colonial conquest in the Americas through catastrophic differential mortality, and was eradicated by a global vaccination campaign in 1980 ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù the only human infectious disease to be deliberately eliminated from nature.

title: Social Class
summary: Social class is an attempt to explain why inequality persists across generations in societies that claim to offer equal opportunity. Whether it does explain this ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù and which of several competing frameworks explains it best ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù is one of the most contested questions in social science.

title: The Social Contract
summary: The political theory that legitimate government rests on agreement ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù explicit or implicit ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù among the people it governs, developed by Hobbes, Locke, and Rousseau into the foundational framework of modern constitutional democracy, and contested ever since on questions of who actually agreed, what was agreed to, and whether the theory can justify political obligation at all.

title: Social Darwinism
summary: The application of evolutionary ideas ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù competition, survival of the fittest, natural selection ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù to human societies, used to justify inequality, empire, and eugenics.

title: Socialism
summary: The family of political and economic ideas holding that production, distribution, and the major resources of society should be owned or regulated collectively rather than by private individuals ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù an idea that has generated revolutionary movements, welfare states, authoritarian regimes, and ongoing debates about the relationship between equality and freedom.

title: Socrates
summary: An Athenian philosopher who wrote nothing but transformed Western philosophy ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù through the practice of public questioning he called elenchus, his conviction that the unexamined life is not worth living, and his trial and execution for impiety and corrupting the youth of Athens, which became the defining image of philosophy as a dangerous practice.

title: The Song Dynasty
summary: The Song Dynasty (960ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™1279 CE) produced more technological and economic innovation than any pre-modern society ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù printing, gunpowder weapons, the magnetic compass, paper money, proto-industrial iron production ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù without any of it compounding into an industrial revolution. Understanding why is one of the central questions in world history.

title: The Songhai Empire
summary: The Songhai Empire (c. 1464ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™1591) was the largest state in West African history ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù controlling the central node of trans-Saharan trade, hosting one of the medieval worldΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβÄû¬Δs largest universities at Timbuktu, and governing a territory larger than Western Europe. Understanding what Songhai was as a political and commercial civilization is the entryΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβÄû¬Δs purpose.

title: Sovereignty
summary: Sovereignty is the claim to final authority within a territory ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù the right to make ultimate decisions, answer to no higher power, and exclude others from intervening. It is not a fact but a claim, and the most revealing cases are the ones where two sovereignty claims collide and neither can yield without ceasing to be sovereign.

title: The Soviet Union under Stalin
summary: The period of Soviet history from Lenin's death through Stalin's own ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù encompassing forced collectivization, the Great Terror, the Second World War, and the Cold War's beginning ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù during which the USSR became an industrial superpower and a totalitarian state responsible for millions of deaths.

title: The Space Race
summary: The superpower competition in spaceflight between the United States and the Soviet Union from roughly 1955 to 1975 ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù beginning with Sputnik's shock in October 1957, cresting with Neil Armstrong's footstep on the Moon in July 1969, and closing with the ApolloΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§≈™Soyuz handshake in 1975 ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù a contest that was simultaneously a proxy of nuclear rivalry, a propaganda battle over which system could master nature, and the founding moment of the technological-managerial state.

title: The Spanish Civil War
summary: The war fought in Spain from 1936 to 1939 between the elected Republican government and a Nationalist military rebellion ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù drawing in fascist Italy and Nazi Germany on one side and the Soviet Union and international volunteers on the other, ending in Franco's dictatorship and functioning as the defining ideological conflict of the 1930s for a generation of European and American writers, intellectuals, and political actors.

title: The Spanish Conquest of the Americas
summary: The decades-long process by which Spanish expeditions toppled the largest states in the Americas ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù the Aztec Triple Alliance in 1521 and the Inca realm after 1532 ΟÉ¬ΔΟΔβÄö¬§ΟΔβ²§¬ù and built an empire across two continents.
